title: The meaning you cannot see
assignment: Review the concepts from this section. If something is unclear, go back to the lesson.
groups.0.title: Names and labels
groups.0.items.0.def: class is a KIND — it can repeat ("all bikes"). id is a NAME — once per page ("this exact one"). Repeat an id and the browser does not crash — it grabs the first and moves on, and half your code silently fails. In doubt — class.
groups.0.items.1.def: class="price", not class="red". Tomorrow the price turns green — "red" on green is a trap for anyone reading the code. A price stays a price, whatever you do to it.
groups.0.items.2.def: One thing id does on its own, no CSS: a link to #top takes you to the thing with that id, no reload. That is why id must be unique — otherwise the browser does not know where to take you.
groups.1.title: The empty boxes
groups.1.items.0.def:
is a block box, is an inline wrap — the same pair as

and . They group so CSS has something to grab. But they mean NOTHING on their own.
groups.1.items.1.def: The danger: it is easy to wrap everything in <div> and tell yourself "it works". It does — but the code no longer says what the page IS. Google hears a group, the reader hears a group, so do you six months later. <div> is used only WHEN THERE IS NO tag.
groups.2.title: The tags of meaning
groups.2.items.0.term: header, nav, main, article, aside, footer
groups.2.items.0.def: <main> is exactly one (the point). <article> is standalone (makes sense lifted out). <aside> is off to the side (not the point). The reader collects them into a list of landmarks and jumps between them. The test: read only the tags aloud — does it sound like a page? Beauty is skin-deep; structure is not.
